top_weaknesses.issue: H2 outline read alone is generic ('The Agent Learns the Business', 'Switching Creates Hidden Risk', 'The Moat Is Earned by Doing the Work') — no domain anchor; H2-skimmer scored 4/10
top_weaknesses.fix: Rewrite H2s to carry concrete payload: 'Operational Memory: What Agents Learn That Databases Don't', 'Why Replacing a Workflow Agent Feels Like Surgery', 'How Workflow Lock-In Is Earned (Outcomes, Not Dark Patterns)'

top_weaknesses.issue: Funding stats ($155M / 61%) are off-thesis (post is about lock-in, not capital flows), low-trust source, anchor mismatches link slug; major credibility drag for HN/Stratechery readers
top_weaknesses.fix: Delete both stats or replace with one sourced sentence; the argument works without them

top_weaknesses.issue: Two external-thesis name-drops (NFX defensibility, Sequoia outcomes) without anchoring to a concrete failure mode; reads as borrowed authority
top_weaknesses.fix: Cut one and replace with a real workflow scenario (support refund flow, AR billing): what the agent learned, what would break in migration, what metric to watch

top_weaknesses.issue: Missing acknowledgement of counter-trend (portability standards, swappable agents); 'strongest switching cost in 2026' is too universal
top_weaknesses.fix: Add one sentence qualifying scope ('in action-taking enterprise AI workflows...') and a counterpoint about portability layers

consensus_summary: Strong thesis and the operator metaphor ('operational surgery') is genuinely shareable, but the H2-only skim fails badly and the off-topic funding stats undermine credibility. Sharpen H2s to carry payload, drop the stats, and swap one name-drop for a concrete migration-risk scenario.
